citation: Adams, M. J., Finch, M., & Gamble, B. (2023). More than a game: Scenario planning, imagination, and the public library's future. Public Library Quarterly, 42(6), 665-687. https://doi.org/10.1080/01616846.2022.2154068
summary:
Adams, Finch, and Gamble examine scenario planning as a strategic-planning method for public libraries facing turbulence, uncertainty, novelty, and ambiguity. The article is centered on Reading Public Library in Pennsylvania and the process it used to plan amid COVID-19, U.S. political polarization, local economic and demographic change, and leadership transition. The authors argue that strategic planning should not focus only on present organizational goals; it should also imagine the contextual future world in which those goals might have to operate.

The paper defines scenarios as storytelling tools derived from current-day realities that present plausible glimpses into future contexts. In the Oxford Scenario Planning Approach used at Reading Public Library, scenarios are not predictions or preferred futures. They are imagined future contexts designed to contrast with current assumptions, stretch perception, expose blind spots, and help decision-makers ask what is happening, what it means, and what they can do. The article therefore treats scenario planning as a form of strategic reframing.

Reading Public Library's context matters to the case. The library serves Reading, Pennsylvania, a post-industrial city with a long library history, financial challenges, poverty, local political complexity, and pandemic disruptions. During the pandemic, RPL expanded digital services, streaming media, online programming, pickup services, and Go Packs distributed through school and recreation partnerships. The article positions these pressures as the setting for a planning process that could not simply extrapolate from stable past conditions.

The scenario process began with virtual workshops and a mapping exercise in which staff and trustees explored the library's business environment, flows of value, partner relationships, and uncertainties affecting the future. The team generated scenarios for 2040, eventually working with three future contexts: Life in the Clouds, The Wild Frontier, and Paris on the Schuylkill. These futures varied in their assumptions about digital life, cooperation, fragmentation, civic flourishing, arts, education, and community relationships. Staff and trustees then used the scenarios to look back at present choices, identifying signs that each future might already be emerging and considering what strategic actions would remain robust across possible futures.

The process produced four strategic tracks: Library Staff Reflecting the Community, Co-designed Programs, Digital Opportunities, and Collection Management. The language of tracks intentionally evoked Reading's railroad history. The article's first-person reflections emphasize that scenario planning helped staff look outward, talk about partners and community forces, imagine RPL serving residents in new ways, and avoid a conventional SWOT process that might have felt too narrow in a turbulent moment. The scenarios also shaped later decisions, including workspace planning, art display policy, digital access efforts, local history digitization, staffing, community art partnerships, and teen internship programs.

The article's title and discussion explicitly connect scenario planning to play. The authors draw on Leorke and Wyatt's idea of the library as playground, then argue that the play of mind in scenario work can extend beyond makerspaces, video gaming events, gamification, and live-action role-play. The scenario workshop becomes a playful heterotopia: a space separated from everyday expectations in which participants can suspend ordinary assumptions, play with future identities, and test strategic actions without real-world consequences. Strategy is described as a form of serious play and conceptual simulation.

For the GBLS project, this source is adjacent but not a direct literature-review addition. It is about using playful scenario methods for internal library strategy, not about delivering game-based library services to patrons, building game collections, designing game programs, teaching through games, or evaluating patron-facing play. Its most relevant insight is conceptual: games and play can shape library organizations not only through public programs but also through planning, imagination, and institutional identity work. However, including it in the main GBLS review would broaden the scope toward general strategic foresight and organizational play rather than the project's focus on game-based library services.

The source is therefore best kept as explicitly excluded literature with a note. It may become useful if the project later adds a section on staff-facing serious play, scenario planning, or gameful strategic design, but it should not currently be used as evidence for GBLS programming or services. Its limitations for the current review are not flaws in the article itself; they are scope limitations. The case is rich, reflective, and library-specific, but its audience and intervention are internal planning stakeholders rather than library patrons or learners.

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY 2023, VOL. 42, NO. 6, 665–687 https://doi.org/10.1080/01616846.2022.2154068

More than a Game: Scenario Planning, Imagination, and the Public Library’s Future
Melissa J. Adams a, Matt Finchb, and Bronwen Gamblec
aExecutive Director, Reading Public Library, Reading, Pennsylvania, USA; bSaïd Business School, University of Oxford, Oxford, UK; cRetired Executive Director, Reading Public Library, Reading, Pennsylvania, USA

ABSTRACT
Strategic planning requires analyzing not only the future goals and trajectory of an organization but also the contextual future world in which that organization will operate. Without being able to travel in time to see what the world will be like in ten, twenty, or even fifty years, organizations often avoid exploring this vital component to strategic planning. There is, however, a way to time travel metaphorically by incorporating scenarios into the strategic planning process. Scenarios are storytelling tools, derived from current-day realities, that present glimpses into the potential chapters of a future that has yet to be written. These scenarios allow organizations to explore how, and even if, their future goals might mesh with the future storylines of their communities and the world at large. Public libraries that use scenarios for strategic planning will have greater flexibility and adaptability to implement change, especially during uncertain times. An examination of a scenario-based approach to strategic planning and the specific scenario-based process utilized at the Reading Public Library illustrates the advantages public libraries can gain by employing scenarios when navigating turbulence and uncertainty.

ARTICLE HISTORY Received July 2022 Accepted November 2022
KEYWORDS Strategic planning; scenario; adaptability; Reading Public Library

Introduction
The unfortunate fact that librarians cannot predict the future with perfect confidence – or travel in time to visit it – makes strategic planning a challenging process. This process is even further complicated during times of uncertainty, when the conditions tomorrow are not guaranteed to be like the conditions today or yesterday. From COVID-19 and the increased domes­ tic political polarization of the United States to emerging global geopolitical conflicts, powerful and hard-to-predict forces are increasingly redrawing the map on which public libraries must operate.
Strategy under such circumstances becomes a matter of “shooting the rapids” (Wack 1985), in the manner of a boat navigating the uneven waters of a fast-moving river. For public libraries as much as any other institution,

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

organizational success and survival may become a matter of insightful antici­ pation, shrewd judgment, flexibility, adaptability, and even the design of novel collaborations or partnerships that may serve to stabilize the business environment.
So-called “TUNA” conditions characterized by Turbulence, Uncertainty, Novelty, and Ambiguity (Ramírez and Wilkinson 2016) may present profound threats to the tasks and even the identity of an institution, but also offer new opportunities which can only be grasped if they are successfully identified. As scenario planning pioneer Pierre Wack put it, citing Richard Rumelt, for an organization to create value, it
must discover the forces at work in its social, technological, and economic world and move to make those forces work for it rather than against it.
When the forces at work are weak, analysis is easy, but opportunities are few. Stable industries offer few chances for important strategic initiatives. When the forces at work are dramatic, analysis is hard but there are very real opportunities for gaining strategic advantage. (Oxford Futures Library 2016)
This article gives an account of the strategic process employed by Reading Public Library (RPL) in Pennsylvania to navigate the turbulence and deep uncertainty of the early 2020s, including the COVID-19 pandemic, the ongoing strife of U.S. domestic politics, and a moment of institutional transi­ tion as one Executive Director departed and a new one assumed the role.
This paper offers a methodological reflection on the scenario-based approach RPL and its consultant took to plan for uncertainty, followed by first-person accounts of the strategy process from the outgoing as well as the incoming Executive Director. The process and its wider implications for strategic planning in libraries and information institutions are then examined and discussed.

Setting the scene in Reading, Pennsylvania
The sons of William Penn laid out the city of Reading in 1748. It is located at the junction of the Schuylkill River and the great Penn-Lebanon Valley. In the 1800s, Reading became an industrial powerhouse with the incorporation of the Philadelphia & Reading Railroad, hauling coal and passengers nationwide. As manufacturing declined, so did Reading’s fortunes. Now a typical “rust belt” town, the area lost 8,000 manufacturing jobs. Notorious in recent years for a 2011 U.S. Census report which identified it as the poorest small city in the nation (Tavernise, 2011), it has since moved down the poverty rankings, slowly shaking off past stigma.
Berks County is known for its fertile farmland, picturesque small towns, and a wide range of outdoor activities. Its prime location – two hours from NYC, one hour from Philadelphia, and three hours from Washington, D.C. – makes

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

667

it ideal for commuters and recreational visitors. The Philadelphia Inquirer presented Berks County as a “microcosm” of Pennsylvania (Brennan, 2020); its conservative suburbs and rural areas contrasting with the liberal-leaning urban center.
RPL began as a subscription library when several prominent men met at Widow Drury’s Tavern in 1763. The library was shuttered and reborn in several incarnations over its 259-year history, facing numerous financial challenges; a 1971 history of the service bore the title The Library That Would Not Die: The Turbulent History of the Reading Public Library (Heizmann, 1971). In 1899, RPL became an official department of the City of Reading and in 1910, Andrew Carnegie funded the building of the current main library facility. RPL opened three additional branches beginning in 1939 with current purpose-built loca­ tions added in the 1970s. All have been renovated in the last six years thanks to public/private partnerships. The early twenty-first century brought the chal­ lenge of unfunded mandates to the library. Jaeger and Bertot suggest that the e-government environment, which includes an increased reliance on e-govern­ ment documents, enabled government “agencies to reduce their costs by off­ loading government information and service functions to public libraries” (2011, p. 104). As Smith notes, “the information landscape is littered with requirements to preserve or deposit data in trustworthy archives, yet there is little funding earmarked to do so” (2007, p. 20). Coupled with significant government funding cuts beginning in 2009 that impacted library services and collections, these types of unfunded mandated requirements necessitated large withdrawals from the RPL trusts just to keep the doors open. In 2008– 2009, unfunded mandates resulted in the decrease in “business hours of 14.5% of public libraries in metropolitan areas” across the nation (Jaeger and Bertot 2011, 103). Likewise, RPL’s hours of operation were reduced at this time in an attempt to offset these new burdens.
While the COVID-19 pandemic forced the physical closure of all public libraries in Pennsylvania, the community of RPL was still able to access many library services including ebooks, online databases, and phone reference assistance. Within two weeks, RPL significantly increased digital streaming media content, extended digital borrowing limits, and provided online pro­ grams through the library’s YouTube and IGTV channels. Lobby Library ordering and pickup services were added when requirements allowed and more than 7,756 Go Packs with free books, STEM, and art activities were distributed through the Reading School District/Reading Recreation Department’s free lunch program while schools were also closed.

Scenario planning for public libraries: a reflection on methodology
As discussed in a previous paper on the RPL scenarios (Finch 2021a), scenario planning is already known within the library sector (see, for example, Finch

668

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

and Ramírez 2018; Hannabuss 2001; Manžuch 2016; O’Connor, Blair, and McConchie 1997; O’Connor and Sidorko 2008; University 2021). Diverse scenario methodologies have been employed in a range of library settings; as noted across other sectors in Spaniol and Rowland (2019)’s review of con­ temporary scholarship on scenario planning, the term “scenario” is widely used but poorly defined. Some approaches elicit preferred or normative future visions, others generate exploratory scenarios validated according to possibi­ lity or plausibility. During the pandemic, even simple ad-hoc scenario pro­ cesses with minimal methodology were reported as providing some value to library leaders (Cowell 2021). Spaniol and Rowland suggest understanding scenarios as a systematized set of comparative narrative descriptions about their users’ external contextual environment, future oriented and plausibly possible. In essence, scenarios are storytelling tools, derived from current-day realities, that present glimpses into the potential chapters of a future that has yet to be written. These scenarios allow organizations to explore how, and even if, their future goals might mesh with the future storylines of their commu­ nities and the world at large.
RPL employed a version of the Oxford Scenario Planning Approach devel­ oped by Ramírez and Wilkinson (2016). Scenario planners working with the Oxford approach seek to manufacture plausible alternative assessments of the future context for a particular issue or enterprise, developed for a specific purpose, and designed to contrast with the way that the future context is currently being framed. Scenarios themselves (imagined future contexts) are clearly distinguished from strategy (the actions which a client or scenario user might take today, informed by the perspective of each future context).
To create Oxford-style scenarios, strategists explore the impact of factors in the broader contextual environment “that is beyond the direct and indirect influence of a strategist” (Ramírez and Wilkinson 2016, 217) on the immediate transactional environment “which one can influence by interacting with the other actors that comprise it” (see Figure 1) (p. 222).
Visions of future transactional environments are developed through collec­ tive iterations of systems mapping and storytelling. This enables a process of reframing and reperception by which the scenarios stretch users’ sense of what is going on around them and what is yet to transpire: each imagined future becomes a vantage point from which to understand the current context afresh, highlighting blind spots, opportunities, and alternative interpretations which may be otherwise unavailable in the present day. From a strategy perspective, Ramírez and Wilkinson suggest, this means that scenarios can help people explore the three areas which Geoffrey Vickers identified as fundamental to sound decision-making: reality, values, and instrumental capacity, or “What’s happening? What does it mean for us? And what can we do about it?” (see Figure 2).

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

669

Figure 1. Example contextual and transactional environments, the Oxford Scenarios Program.
Answering these questions from the distinct vantage points of each scenario offers insights unavailable through the standard frame of the present, enrich­ ing the essential components of strategy set out by Rumelt: “a diagnosis of the situation, the choice of an overall guiding policy, and the design of coherent action” (Rumelt 2011, 268).
This approach complements the current vogue for evidence-based decisionmaking within library and information science: given that data and evidence cannot be gathered from events which haven’t happened yet, and in the memorable phrase of Ged Davis “a trend is a trend until it bends”; scenarios enable us to manufacture future contexts which can inform decision-making in times of unpredictable uncertainty. As the Bodleian Libraries’ Frankie Wilson (2019) put it, sometimes organizations need to make strategic deci­ sions which existing evidence seems to tell them not to make.
Scoblic (2020) argues that, when past precedent is not a useful guide to what tomorrow will hold, scenarios can let us “learn from the future by simulating experiences – a process that reduces bias and renders us more perceptive, flexible, and adaptable to environmental change.” For Burt and Nair (2020), the benefit of scenarios may include “unlearning” as well as learning: making

670

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

Figure 2. Vickers’ “three judgments,” adapted from Ramírez and Wilkinson (2016). Three circles in a triangular arrangement, overlapping at the center in the manner of a Venn diagram. They are labeled “Instrumental: What Can We Do?,” “Reality: What is Going On?,” and “Values: What Does It Mean For Us?.” Where the circles overlap, a red triangle is superimposed; it is labeled “Decision-making.”
use of scenarios’ provisional and speculative quality to enable “letting go or relaxing the rigidities of previously held assumptions and beliefs, rather than forgetting them” (p. 2).
Such assumptions and beliefs may even reach to the fundamentals of an institution’s identity. This is particularly for libraries; as has been written elsewhere regarding the work in Reading (Finch 2021b),
Imagining future contexts which were not the ones we expected or assumed can also have implications for organizational identity. In a changed world, we may have to become something which we weren’t - or didn’t see ourselves as - in times before. This could be a particular challenge for long-standing institutions like the library: in so many languages, the institution’s very name has roots in ancient words for the book, Latin liber and Greek biblion - and often discussions about the library’s future become trapped in stale debates over the much-predicted “death of the book”.

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

671

As Lang (2022) notes, there is a “temporal mismatch” between organizational identity, which is inherited from the past, and strategy, which is oriented toward the future. Scenario-based strategic approaches create the opportunity for organizations and their members to play with alternative identities, allow­ ing “new identity possibilities to be considered without the current one being imperiled” as they ask: Who might we have to become in each of these futures?
In the Reading scenario planning process, the “temporal mismatch” was addressed through a series of slides showing a timeline of the library’s history across various incarnations from 1763 to the present (Finch 2022). In addition, strategic attentiveness to the library’s role within a wider ecosystem of rela­ tionships was deepened by partially integrating the scenarios with Ramírez and Mannervik’s (2016) value-creating systems approach (VCS). VCS inves­ tigates how value of different kinds is co-created by multiple actors within systems of use. VCS strategy becomes a matter of intentionally designing offerings which link actors together to co-create value; collaboration becomes as important as competition, and the focus is on the overall well-being of the system.
Oxford-style scenarios integrate with the VCS methodology by offering a way to explore and assess the future contexts which a given system of value creation might inhabit. Future contexts, with different ecosystems, may present new challenges or opportunities for a current or proposed system: will the same value be created by the same actors in times to come?
Through the VCS process, contextual factors might prove to be amenable to influence through collaboration with other actors, and contextual uncertain­ ties might thus be transformed into manageable transactions. At RPL, while it was not possible to implement a full VCS process, it was hoped that an ecosystemic and future-oriented perspective on strategy would support the library in successfully addressing such powerful forces beyond its control, from the COVID-19 pandemic to ongoing political polarization in the local community. To this end, three scenarios were generated for the year 2040, exploring how the library’s ecosystem might come to be comprised of different actors, relationships, and values in futures characterized by differing degrees of cooperation or fragmentation in civic life, and differences in the degree to which that life remained “in-person,” or migrated to fully digital online spaces.

The outgoing director’s story: working with the future to understand the present
If RPL had begun the traditional strategic planning process of SWOT analysis, surveys, and board/staff workshops that should have occurred in 2019, the COVID-19 pandemic may have rendered it obsolete as soon as it was finished. The strategic planning committee needed a process that gave them the

672

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

opportunity to look into the future and imagine RPL serving residents in new and challenging ways.
While working from home during the lockdown, the Executive Director attended a series of three virtual workshops sponsored by the Bureau of Library Development at the Pennsylvania Office of Commonwealth Libraries and facilitated by Dr Matthew Finch linking in from his London office. The series was eye-opening and exciting, and created sense that Dr Matthew Finch’s Scenario and Foresight Planning method could be an opportunity for RPL to create an entirely new plan to confidently move the library into the unknown rather than “back to normal.”
Fast forward to 2021, after enduring through 2020s COVID-19 challenges, work with Dr Matthew Finch began. A team of key library staff and the chair of the board’s Strategic Planning Committee spent many hours focusing on their goals. Committee members created an online mural map of the library’s business environment, including flows of value to and from the library, and uncertainties which might affect the environment of the future (see Figure 3). The map was dynamic, which allowed the committee members to easily make additions and changes. This process gave participants a clear understanding of the extensive web of collaboration, service, and transaction which shapes library work at every level from customer to community to state and federal mandates.
The process of translating this dynamic mural map to arrive at the final scenarios of Life in the Clouds, The Wild Frontier, and Paris on the Schuylkill began by breaking into small discussion groups. These small groups examined the multitude of current challenges facing RPL by charting daily interactions and experiences, and determining if these could affect the future of the library. It became clear that both local and national events affect RPL on a practical and personal level. The groups discussed:

● The opioid crisis which leads to addiction, hopelessness, and turf gang violence
● The wealth gap in Berks County, lack of education, and access to employment
● Social media and the power of “influencers” while at the same time a decline in traditional local news outlets in print, radio, and television
● Uneven access to health-care services with language barriers, lack of transportation, and feeling unsafe to ask for help
● Divisive politics, who holds political power, political labels, and an endless focus on reelection
● Social injustice and unrest which highlights segregation by neighborhood ● Taxes, what they provide, and politicians’ reluctance to raise them ● Huge local, state, and national spending on the court system and incar­
ceration which limits funds for social institutions such as libraries ● Inertia – a Berks County specialty

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

673

Figure 3. Diagram of RPL’s transactional environment including value mapping, Reading Public Library, 2021.

674

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

Building on the work of the small groups, the planning committee dis­ cussed, argued, and refined the above and agreed on two key uncertainties to structure the scenario set on an X- and Y-axis; two key factors that could transform the library environment in the most challenging and uncertain ways in the future. The factors selected were the degree of Cooperation or Fragmentation in society and the degree to which civic life was conducted in a Digital Space or in Physical presence. The committee chose a time horizon of 2040 to best explore the chosen factors. Twenty years into the future was far enough out to explore how current dynamics may play out on their key uncertainties and how their chosen scenarios could diverge as time moves forward. It felt like a combination of science fiction, fantasy, and news head­ lines! The committee divided into four “rooms,” each taking a section of the Xand Y-axis which was visible to all on the mural map, and fleshing out what each future might look like twenty years hence (see Figure 4).

● Fragmented & Digital was labeled the Digital Wild West ● Cooperation & Digital was labeled Big Brother/Brave New World ● Cooperation and Physical was labeled Paris on the Schuylkill ● Fragmented and Physical was labeled Bunker Mentality

Ramírez and Wilkinson emphasize that scenario planning in this method is an iterative process (2016, p. 4). As the committee envisioned life in Reading and Berks County, Pennsylvania, in 2040, they decided that the power of

Figure 4. The Reading scenarios grid, Reading Public Library, 2021.

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

675

fragmentation as factor was so great, and played out so similarly in the scenarios explored, that it was more useful to combine the Digital Wild West and Bunker Mentality into one, thus identifying and creating three scenarios:

Life in the Clouds
The Wild Frontier Paris on the Schuylkill. The Life in the Clouds scenario imagined a future in which social and economic life migrated almost entirely online in the next twenty years. Privacy gave way to Big Data, but there were also new forms of digital democracy and the recognition that American society could not survive without cooperation. RPL evolved into a “Public Informatics Commission,” regulating digital equity, and helping residents participate fully in life online.
The second scenario, The Wild Frontier, saw a dramatic fragmentation of civic life in which the basis for trust and dialog had all but disintegrated. Residents retreated both online and offline into “bunkers” defined by shared common values and the exclusion of outsiders. This scenario presented grave challenges to the notion of library neutrality and the library’s dependence on local, county, and state tax income.
The final scenario, Paris on the Schuylkill, imagined a new flowering of civil society. In this world, people pulled together for the sake of their children’s futures. Universal Basic Income and growth in higher education turned Reading into a city of artists and scholars, a sought-after destination for arts and culture. The RPL of this world was an integral partner with local uni­ versities and served as an incubator of the arts and innovation.
The committee used the three scenarios to examine RPL’s situation in 2021. What signs were there that any of these futures might emerge? What other possibilities had this work exposed? How would people in any of these scenarios look back at the choices made today? There were exciting, impromptu discussions in library break rooms, hallways, board meetings, and staff meetings to ensure everyone was part of the process. Additionally, each person on the committee took their work to the community partners they identified on the original mural map for their perspective on Berks County’s future. The process forced everyone to look up from the day-to-day and question their own assumptions. After gathering input, it was time to define RPL’s plans for the future by developing “railroad tracks,” an appropriate label to use for the birthplace of the Reading Railroad. The committee members met in person twice to facilitate animated discussions and distilled the mural map and three scenarios into four tracks:
● Co-designed Programs ● Intentional Partnerships

676

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

● Staff Reflects the Community ● Create Digital Opportunities

For each track, the committee developed tasks and metrics: What steps do we take? How do we measure progress? These reflected the committee’s insight that across all scenarios RPL must adapt
to community needs, doing things with residents rather than to them. The tracks can flex in case RPL needed to adjust its strategy as the future unfolds, while moving to a more proactive and engaged approach will ensure resilient library services that provide value in a changing world.
Because RPL engaged in the scenario planning process in the midst of the pandemic, the timeline stretched to six months. The committee made a conscious decision not to control the timing in order to facilitate the free flow of ideas and conversation. By taking their time, they found “ah-ha” moments that questioned their assumptions or propelled them forward. In particular, small group discus­ sions on community-led programs revolved around the committee’s anxiety about “giving up control” when inviting residents to create and lead programming. A deeper dive into providing a pathway for staff to learn new skills and take on new roles provided a host of ideas to aid both recruitment of new staff and training of current staff.
The consultant ensured inclusive views and participation. He devised templates in a shared online whiteboard which helped everyone complete each step along the way in a logical progression. scenario planning forced library personnel to look far beyond the usual three-to-five-year plan. This process was so successful with the planning team because it was more than an intellectual exercise. The group was passionate about the library’s partners, challenges, and the potential positive impact in the community. It was a visceral process which included everyone’s emotions and personalities; as Ramírez and Wilkinson emphasize (2016, p. 109), strategizing in this way is ”a social process which uses a mix of critical, creative, and analytical thinking”.
When envisioning life far into the future, RPL personnel widened their vision, moving out of the tunnel, which then allowed them to reel the future back into a timeframe in which the next steps were based on a clear understanding of where and how the library could meet the needs of constituents. At this point, when the scenario planning is finished and the final written draft is approved by the board, the next concern becomes how to convey this unique process and plan to com­ munity partners, government bodies, and patrons. While the written plan is primarily an internal document, the library needed something succinct and enga­ ging for the public. To remedy this, the library hired local graphic artist Craig Schaffer to develop an infographic that illustrated the process, the scenarios in 2040, and the four railroad tracks (see Figure 5).

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

677

Figure 5. Infographic for the RPL 2022–2024 Strategic Plan, Reading Public Library.

678

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

After going to 2040 and back again, how will RPL’s 2022–2024 scenariobased Strategic Plan be used? Will it be translated into action? Because library staff and board were involved with the entire process, it does not solely rest on the shoulders of the Executive Director. This point was crucial for a seamless transition when navigating a retirement and the subsequent hiring of a new Executive Director at the end of 2021. RPL’s new Executive Director, library staff, and the board of trustees all share the responsibility of moving the plan forward.

The incoming director’s story: we’ve been to 2040, now what?
After the strategic planning process is complete, the real challenge of how to translate that plan into action begins. During the planning process, four tracks were identified as goals to focus on Co-designed Programs, Intentional Partnerships, Library Staff Reflects the Community, and Create Digital Opportunities.
Each of these four tracks were developed to be relevant in moving the library forward regardless of which scenario comes to pass. This is why one way to approach implementing the strategic plan would be to focus only on the resulting tracks and goals of the plan – treating those goals in isolation from the rest of the strategic plan. While the library would likely still proceed forward with an approach that focuses solely on the tracks, any progress would not benefit from the focus and context that the scenarios provide.
Working with scenarios ensures that strategic decisions are durable enough to succeed in each potential future (Lang and Whittington 2022). Ultimately, public libraries do not operate in silos; they are, or ideally should be, inter­ woven into the fabric of their extended communities. Using the scenarios beyond the planning stage reminds libraries “to stop thinking about strategy simply as the making of plans for the future” and to start learning where their communities are headed and applying that to making decisions and taking actions (Lang and Whittington 2022).
For example, the decision about how to repurpose an empty Main Library workspace was informed by not just the tracks but also by the three scenarios. The room could have been turned into a regular programming space, used for storage, or transformed into another office, to name some of the many options staff considered. However, in the end, the decision was made to turn it into a Maker Space, and to create renovation plans that include asking teens who will be using the space to help with design and decorating decisions. This project allows the library to work toward the Co-designed Programs and the Digital Opportunities tracks, while also considering the three scenarios: it provides the perfect opportunity to actively involve the community (The Wild Frontier), to help residents participate in life online (Life in the

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

679

Clouds), and to serve as an incubator for STEAM innovations (Paris on the Schuylkill).
One of the stumbling blocks to navigating scenarios is the tendency for staff to gravitate toward just one of the possible scenarios. In this case, staff and board members tend to focus on Paris on the Schuylkill to the exclusion of the others. On one hand, it makes sense to work toward an ideal world and hope actions shape the future; however, ignoring the less desirable possibilities may make the institution less malleable to reality. Additionally, smaller-scale aspects of each scenario are already at play in the current world, making it even more vital that they aren’t forgotten.
While The Wild Frontier appears to be ripped from the pages of a dystopian novel, it may in fact be what Reading looks like in 2040. If library staff and board aren’t keeping that possibility in mind while making decisions, they may find the library unprepared and unable to effectively function. For example, libraries across the United States are seeing an increase in book challenges due in large part to the fragmentation of society into divergent and self-isolating groups. If society continues to fragment and these conflicting groups become even more insular, book challenges and bans will only increase in frequency and severity. Keeping this possibility in mind when revising policies will help inform what policies are needed, as well as the content and wording within policies. With The Wild Frontier as a possibility for the future, when the library staff began working on a plan to display community artwork, leadership decided an Art Display Policy was required. This policy anticipates this undesirable future and prepares the library for exclusionary tactics by specify­ ing guidelines for submission, how artwork will be selected, and what steps will be taken if there is a challenge.
Librarians often are plagued by a society that views libraries as dinosaurs and argues, ‘Who needs libraries when you have Google?’ Attending to the Life in the Clouds scenario offers a realistic way forward, as it allows libraries to directly address how to stay relevant in an entirely digital world. Life in the Clouds may at first seem like something out of a Science Fiction novel; however, during the COVID-19 pandemic society relied extensively on digital resources. This dependence on the online world hasn’t decreased even though society is arguably ‘returning to normal.’ With an eye toward this possible future, the library continues to offer virtual programming, and to explore ways to provide greater digital access. Projects to create more equitable access have shifted higher on the library’s to-do list, including plans to digitize a local history collection, to hire a digital literacy instructor, and to increase the WiFi reach beyond library buildings. With a mind toward Life in the Clouds, the library has been exploring how to provide more equitable digital access to the local newspaper’s current and archival editions. This made the decision to add a digital image-rich version of the current edition to the library website an easy

680

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

one and was the impetus for developing plans to do the same with the archival editions.
The final scenario identified is Paris on the Schuylkill, which might on one hand be considered an idealized utopian future and the golden age of Reading. In this futuristic world, everyone works together in unity and the results are a thriving community excelling in art, education, economy, and culture. In this possible future, the library would be a key component of this progress and knowing this has helped influence numerous decisions from hiring practices to programming. The library has prioritized finding ways for staff to be more reflective of the community and providing pathways to increase community members’ training and future employment. This involves reexamining job descriptions, lowering education requirements when possible, partnering with other organizations to create internships for high school and college students, and exploring educational reimbursement programs to foster pro­ motion within. All of these are strategic actions RPL is now taking.
Recently, the library decided to take part in a City of Reading summer internship program for high school students. Taking part of this program will allow community members to see themselves in the staff of the library and will give several teens valuable experience for their future. The library is addition­ ally working with local colleges and Pennsylvania CareerLink to establish a college-age internship program to create another employment path for residents. Reading Area Community College, Albright University, and the new addition of Alvernia University’s CollegeTowne have primed the City of Reading for Paris on the Schuylkill. With improvements to the city like CollegeTowne, the newly unveiled plans for a Reading High School STEM satellite, and the ongoing rehabilitation of downtown Reading’s iconic old buildings by the developer Alan Shunman, the seeds for growing this future scenario are already in place, provided RPL takes the initiative to cultivate partnerships and invite themselves to the table.
However, potential challenges do exist in this quasi-utopian future that would have to be managed, such as navigating the possible loss of identity or autonomy that could result from partnering or merging with larger entities, like a local university or other cultural institution. Partnerships need to be carefully managed and evaluated to keep the scales of give and take balanced; partnership agreements between RPL and any organization should be mutually beneficial and clearly defined.
A current example is RPL’s partnership with Millersville University for an Artist in Residence program. To start, this partnership required each partner to contribute 50 percent of the costs related to the project, with the library’s financial portion made possible by a PA Humanities grant. RPL also must provide the local artists a studio space at the library for six weeks to work on their art specialty. In return, the library gains the ability to work toward several tracks of RPL’s strategic plan: Library Staff Reflecting the Community and

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

681

Intentional Partnerships. These local artists-in-residence will be able to reflect the community RPL is serving, allowing residents to see themselves in library staff and volunteers, enhancing RPL’s ability to truly attract and maintain relationships with broader portions of the community. It will not only serve this purpose but will also introduce these artists to the library’s culture, possibly setting them on a professional track that includes library work. This is an important advancement in RPL’s approach to cultural education and expression, and will make the library more accessible to community members. Millersville University equally benefits in this partnership with RPL. It allows them to further their mission with the South Central PaARTners, which is to “foster creativity, learning and understanding through diverse events and collaborations” by connecting the community with the essential art experi­ ences (Millersville University). A well thought out plan, a written contract, and guidelines from grants and other partnering organizations all help to keep the project in focus and mutually valuable.
Another thing to keep in mind when working with these scenarios is that while each scenario is plausible, the real-life future in the year 2040 might be a combination of two or more scenarios, or possibly something a little differ­ ent. They are ultimately only plausible possibilities, not concrete alternative timelines. Therefore, one of the best ways to use them is as reminders that libraries are part of a larger world and need to be proactive to the shifts taking place in that world. Using scenarios to inform strategic decisions reminds staff and board members to keep their eye on the ways in which their community and the world are changing. This is what will make pivoting when faced with an unexpected shift that much easier. Additionally, using scenarios can only enhance the relevancy of library services to stakeholders. Focusing outward will ensure that public libraries never become dinosaurs but remain chame­ leons, with an almost 360-degree view of the world around them and the ability to physically adapt to whatever future they encounter.

Discussion and implications: the library as strategic playground
In The Library as Playground, Leorke and Wyatt (2022) explore the notion of “the library as play; that is, as a space and institutional order innately imbued with playful qualities” (p. 17). Beyond the turn toward makerspaces, video­ gaming events, gamification, and live-action role-play in public library set­ tings, the authors argue that, at a deeper level, libraries “invite an imaginative play of the mind that is absolutely inimitable to their spaces” (p. 17) and that, far from ending with programs and collections, games and play in public libraries impact “virtually every aspect of their design, spaces, milieu, manage­ ment, and wider institutional role” (p. 61). They propose that the

682

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

various “magic circles” of play in libraries are [. . .] expanding and becoming permeable. Various types of games and gaming programs are pervading the library. They are expanding play in the library temporally, spatially, and socially in the process - altering its opening hours, bleeding across designated zones, and facilitating spontaneous encounters. (p. 90)

The example of RPL suggests that this could even include the use of scenarios to build strategy in the public library. The scenarios, as plausible rather than predicted or desired futures, offered the opportunity for participants in the planning process to play with expectations, hopes, fears, and desires, in a strategically consequential game of “What if?”
Part of the appeal of working with RPL for the consultant on the scenario process, was the clear willingness of the institution to engage in playful moves to achieve their goals and to allow playful behavior from staff: from a Chariots of Fire-style “torch run” video which he viewed prior to accepting the contract (Reading Public Library 2017) to the use of black bunting draped on empty shelves to highlight the impact of proposed funding cuts in 2009 (Thomas 2013). He had previously developed playful approaches to strategy through the creation of the “Library Island” role-playing game (Finch 2019a; discussed in Finch and Ramírez 2018).
As Ramírez and Drevon (2005) put it, in such playful spaces “the status quo can be suspended or temporarily bracketed, freeing thinking from established here and now constraints and allowing one to look at one’s current situation from an alternative point of view” (p. 197). This resonates well with Leorke and Wyatt’s characterization of play in the library as “initiated through a temporary suspension of everyday codes of conduct, and a conscious deci­ sion to open the imagination and the intellect to a new order defined by different terms for relating and being” (p. 111). The scenario workshop itself becomes a “heterotopia,” a playful space separated from everyday norms and expectations in order that those designing the library’s strategy might think differently about Vickers’ three questions: “What’s happening? What does it mean for us? And what can we do about it?”
Strategy itself can be understood as a form of “serious play” (Roos, Victor, and Statler 2004; Statler, Heracleous, and Jacobs 2011); as Lang (2022) puts it, “Running conceptual simulations constitutes a form of ‘play’ that enables new identity possibilities to be considered without the current one being imperiled and for new strategic actions to be tried free of real-world consequences” (p. 13). As Korotov (2016, p. 108) puts it,

Transitional spaces create opportunities for play and experimentation, help the people inside them overcome change-related anxieties, offer novelty that gives an extra boost to desire for changing the self, impose certain rules and structures that are counternormative to what exists in the world beyond the transitional space, set psychological, temporal and spatial boundaries, and offer a menu of guiding figures to people inside the

PUBLIC LIBRARY QUARTERLY

683

space. All these elements together create the potential for identity experimentation and renewal.
Lang and Whittington (2022) propose that such work might usefully yield strategic “playbooks” like those used by coaches in American football:
Anticipating a variety of plausible futures, leaders generate a broad repertoire of strategic initiatives and responses ahead of time, and in so doing create a playbook that makes them able to adapt and pivot their organizations quickly and successfully as circum­ stances change. To execute strategy in an increasingly complex and dynamic context, plans alone are not enough.
RPL’s “railroad tracks” share some of the flexibility which Lang and Whittington see in the strategic “playbook.” As recounted elsewhere (Finch 2021a), RPL’s areas of strategic focus were
initially labeled as “pillars” but subsequently renamed “tracks”. This honored Reading’s tradition as a railroad town, but also was a significant metaphor: a track, as it was being laid, might have to veer to accommodate features of the landscape on the way to its destination; it might be expected to bear different loads and different kinds of rolling stock over the generations; it might also carry people and goods in both directions. “A pillar,” one of the planning team said wryly, “just stands there and keeps the old structure from falling down.”
Ramírez and Drevon (2005) argue that not only do scenario workshops serve as “transitional spaces” where change can be safely explored and managed, but “the scenarios produced [. . .] are themselves also objects that can be used transitionally to enhance change” (p. 211). It is significant in RPL’s case that the scenarios were commissioned by the outgoing Executive Director and then used enthusiastically by her replacement. The scenarios served as an ongoing space of strategic conversation, discussion, and debate about RPL’s future which bridged the period of transition and smoothed the leadership succes­ sion. A previous case study in scenario planning for an information institu­ tion, the European Patent Office (EPO) which manages one of the largest civilian databases in the world, also involved a leadership transition like that at RPL, where incoming EPO President Alison Brimelow “embraced” scenarios commissioned by her predecessor and “gave them wider internal as well as external distribution” (Ramírez and Wilkinson 2016, 205).
The RPL experience highlights future opportunities for research into the ways in which the playfulness which Leorke and Wyatt identify in the contemporary public library might shape how such libraries strategize. Finch (2021c) has previously explored how scenarios can be used to support libraries with workforce planning in times of uncertainty, and future research is also possible into the ways in which succession planning and staff transitions might be facilitated by the use of scenarios as transitional objects. Finally, the distinctive characteristics of how strategy work is carried out in an information institution such as a library merit further exploration.

684

M. J. ADAMS ET AL.

To the extent that scenario planning involves enabling users to perceive and work with “exformation” (Finch and Mahon 2022) – information that was previously “framed out” by expectations or assumptions – the particular capabilities and interests of information professionals might prove to be especially well suited to this approach to strategy. In times to come, as considered elsewhere (Finch 2019b; Finch and Ramírez 2018), could public libraries even be tasked with devising open, public scenarios not just to inform their own strategic work, but to support the communities they serve with major decisions?

Conclusion
RPL is no stranger to failure. From 1763 to 1899, when it became a department of the City of Reading, this people’s library opened and closed five times. The library’s identity and role have changed over its 259-year history from a German and English language subscription library to a hub for computers, high tech meeting rooms, digital streaming media, and specialized Spanish Language collections. It is the library’s relationship with the community and its needs which sustained RPL during challenging times and will continue to be the focus of the future.
The ability to change both identity and strategy is key. By using the Oxford Scenario Planning Approach, RPL took a long-range look at possible RPL identities with a willingness to suspend beliefs about what a public library is and should be. Yet RPL’s concern with the future was firmly rooted in the real issues and decisions faced in the present. The past, too, was a resource; by reacquainting themselves with the obstacles, failures, and successes of RPL in times gone by, and the ways in which the library had already evolved and adapted to each era’s challenges, the strategic planning committee was able to draw on the historic grit and determination which enabled RPL to reinvent itself repeatedly.
Funding has always been, and will likely always be, an issue. Additionally, diverse and conflicting opinions about library collections and programs, and access to them, are rising rapidly in the United States. However, the scenarios and tracks, developed from this approach to strategic planning, provide options to meet future challenges with flexibility and courage. The Oxford Scenario Planning Approach empowers RPL to look uncertainty dead in the eye and meet future challenges head on.
